does: This paper proposes an adversarial gamification framework for learning robot safety policies through synthetic scenario generation. The core method models safety learning as a competitive game where a Red Team actively constructs hazardous situations and failure modes while a Blue Team iteratively develops and refines safety policies to prevent them. Rather than relying on random simulation or exhaustive manual enumeration of edge cases, this approach leverages adversarial interaction to efficiently discover high-risk scenarios that reveal critical safety vulnerabilities. The work combines classical risk modeling techniques with adversarial scenario generation and contemporary machine learning methods, yielding a problem formulation and proposed solution architecture for embedding safety into physical AI systems deployed in complex real-world environments.
connects: This work builds on decades of adversarial learning research, particularly adversarial training paradigms that have proven effective in robustness research for both vision and control domains. It extends classical systems safety and hazard analysis methods—such as failure mode identification and risk assessment—by automating the discovery of edge cases through learned adversarial agents rather than manual expert enumeration. The approach also connects to recent work on scenario-based testing and automated stress-testing of autonomous systems, as well as multi-agent reinforcement learning frameworks where competing objectives drive exploration. It responds to the longstanding challenge in robot safety of identifying and addressing corner cases and long-tail failure modes that are statistically rare but potentially catastrophic.
enables: This framework opens pathways for scalable, automated discovery of safety-critical edge cases in physical AI systems without requiring exhaustive manual analysis. Future work could leverage this method to systematically build comprehensive safety case documentation and certification arguments for autonomous systems in regulated domains. The adversarial scenario generation approach could extend to learning from real-world incidents by reverse-engineering failure modes, or integrate with formal verification methods to bridge learned policies with provable safety guarantees. The framework also enables more efficient sim-to-real transfer by focusing data collection and real-world testing on scenarios identified as most safety-critical through adversarial play, ultimately reducing the sample complexity of deploying robots safely in unpredictable human environments.
